query: association between ERAP2 and birdshot chorioretinopathy

retrieved_docs.title: Birdshot Retinopathy -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Recent research has demonstrated that the HLA-A29 gene itself is central to the pathogenesis of the disease. [8] [9] [10] [11] It is thought that the enzyme ERAP2 may contribute to the genetic mutation. [11] This enzyme is thought to be part of antigen processing for presentation by class I MHC molecule. [12] This has been recognized in a number of other conditions, such as Crohn disease and psoriasis. There is strong evidence of selective antigen processing by ERAP2 with HLA-A29 enabling a unique immunogenic signal leading to BCR. [10] There is continuing debate as to whether BCR is primarily a disease of the choroid or the retina. The indistinct appearance of the lesions, lack of associated RPE pigmentary changes, and the angiographic features of the lesions suggest these lesions are located in the deep choroidal stroma and are associated with the choroidal veins. It is important to recognize that the retinal and choroidal changes are not necessarily concordant and that this reflects our ability to monitor the disease and may reflect different aspects of its pathogenesis.

retrieved_docs.title: Chorioretinitis -- Pathophysiology -- Non-infective posterior uveitis without systemic illness (white dot syndromes)
retrieved_docs.content: Birdshot chorioretinopathy (BSCR) /vitiliginous choroiditis: More than 90% of the patients with BCR are HLA-A29 positive. The term “birdshot” is because the lesions scattered around the optic disc and radiate to the equator resembling the shotgun scatter of birdshot. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, MHC Class I -- Clinical Significance -- Birdshot Chorioretinopathy
retrieved_docs.content: Birdshot chorioretinopathy, a form of posterior uveitis, correlates with HLA-A29 , with 85% to 97.5% of those diagnosed carrying the allele. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Birdshot Retinopathy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of this condition remains unclear, but it is thought to be related to an autoimmune disease. It may be linked to HLA-A29 due to the presence of this marker in an overwhelming percentage of cases, but triggers for the autoimmune disease remain unclear.

retrieved_docs.title: Birdshot Retinopathy -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Birdshot chorioretinopathy is a progressive disease with the potential for significant visual impairment due to anatomical and functional complications.

retrieved_docs.title: White Dot Syndromes -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of the white dot syndromes remains unknown. Several entities are associated with a viral prodrome, suggesting a potential viral or infectious etiology. Like most autoimmune conditions, an unknown trigger is thought to precipitate an inflammatory or autoimmune process in the posterior eye. [4] The white dot syndromes represent a primary ocular process and are not associated with systemic inflammatory or autoimmune disease. An increased prevalence of such conditions has been demonstrated in several subgroups of patients with white dot syndrome (ie, AZOOR). [5] Birdshot chorioretinopathy is highly associated with the presence of the HLA-A29 haplotype. [6] This correlation is so prominent that the absence of this haplotype, even within typical clinical features, should prompt consideration of an alternative diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Chorioretinitis -- Etiology -- Noninfectious etiology without any systemic association:
retrieved_docs.content: Multifocal choroiditis and panuveitis Punctate inner choroidopathy Multiple evanescent white dots syndrome Unilateral acute idiopathic maculopathy Birdshot choroidopathy Serpiginous choroidopathy

retrieved_docs.title: White Dot Syndromes -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Birdshot chorioretinopathy: middle-aged patients (age 40 to 60 years), women > men, bilateral, difficulty with night vision, multiple cream-colored lesions in a birdshot style, radiating pattern nasally predominant, associated vitritis, CME, and retinal vasculitis, highly correlated with the presence of HLA-A29, “quenching” on FA, guarded visual prognosis, treatment generally required [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Retinitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Retinitis  may be idiopathic or related to infectious, inflammatory, or underlying genetic causes. Posterior uveitis is associated with various human leukocyte antigen (HLA) phenotypes. Birdshot chorioretinitis, linked to HLA-29 in 95% of patients, manifests with chorioretinal lesions resembling birdshot pellets. Uveitis and inflammatory bowel disease co-occur in 45% of patients with HLA-B27. Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada syndrome exhibits a robust HLA class II association, particularly with the HLA-D locus and DR4 allele.

retrieved_docs.title: Birdshot Retinopathy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology of birdshot chorioretinopathy. Describe the appropriate evaluation of patients with suspected birdshot chorioretinopathy. Review the treatment and management options available for birdshot chorioretinopathy. Outline interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance birdshot chorioretinopathy and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Cat Eye Syndrome (Schmid-Fraccaro Syndrome) -- Etiology -- Dosage-Sensitive Candidate Genes
retrieved_docs.content: Several genes within the duplicated critical region have been implicated in the pathogenesis of CES: CECR1 (ADA2) : This gene encodes adenosine deaminase 2; dysregulated activity may contribute to vascular abnormalities and immune dysfunction. CECR2 : This gene functions as a chromatin remodeling factor; overexpression may disrupt the development of neural crest–derived tissues. SLC7A4 : This gene encodes a cationic amino acid transporter; potentially involved in renal and gastrointestinal morphogenesis. TXNRD2 : This gene encodes a mitochondrial thioredoxin reductase; dosage changes may affect cellular oxidative balance. [16]

retrieved_docs.content: Punctate inner choroidopathy Serpiginous choroidopathy Multifocal choroiditis and panuveitis Subretinal fibrosis and uveitis Multiple evanescent white dot syndrome Acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy Birdshot chorioretinopathy

retrieved_docs.title: Birdshot Retinopathy -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Birdshot chorioretinopathy is an unusual cause of ocular inflammation, and obtaining reliable incidence and prevalence data is difficult due to the rarity of the disease. Studies from Europe and the United States report that BCR accounts for between 0.5% and 1.5% of the uveitis cases seen in specialist uveitis practices. [3] [4] BCR is predominantly seen in the middle-aged, and some reports appear to be more common in females. In a systematic review from 2005, Shah et al. reported a mean age of disease onset of 53.0 years (512 patients), and a 54.1 % female preponderance (522 patients). [5] Birdshot chorioretinopathy is most prevalent in White populations, is most commonly diagnosed in people of Northern European ancestry, with only the occasional case report of BCR in Latino-Hispanic, African-American, and Japanese people. [6] The ethnic distribution is relevant to HLA-A29. HLA-A29 subtypes can be observed in many patients, with each subtype having a different prevalence. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: White Dot Syndromes -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for most white dot syndromes (ARPE, MEWDS, APMPPE, AZOOR) is good, with most patients experiencing a benign and self-limited course with an eventual return to or near baseline vision. [25] [26] [27] [26] The persistence of various visual phenomena, such as scotoma and photopsia, may occur in a small percentage of such patients. [26] Several white dot syndromes (MCP, birdshot chorioretinopathy, serpiginous choroiditis) demonstrate a more persistent and progressive clinical course and may result in moderate to severe visual decrement. [28] [29] [30] [28] Therefore, early identification and management of these conditions are paramount.

retrieved_docs.title: Central Serous Chorioretinopathy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Association between H. pylori infection, peptic ulcer disease and CSCR has been reported in some studies. [22] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Aniridia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Aniridia 1 or AN1 (OMIM # 106210)- This results from heterozygous mutation of the PAX6 gene at chromosome 11p13. It is inherited autosomal dominantly, and also includes late-onset corneal dystrophy and congenital cataracts. Other associations include Peters anomaly, reduced smell sensation, hypoplasia of corpus callosum and/or anterior commissure, and the absence of the pineal gland. Aniridia 2 (AN2) (OMIM # 617141, autosomal dominant) results from the mutation of a cis-regulatory element of the PAX6 gene. This regulatory region is at the intron of the adjacent ELP4 gene (11p13). Aniridia 3 (AN3) (OMIM # 617142, autosomal dominant) is due to a mutation in the TRIM44 gene (11p13). Gillespie syndrome (OMIM # 206700) occurs due to a mutation of the ITPR1 gene at chromosome 3p26.1. Research has suggested a probable linkage of autosomal dominant aniridia and acid phosphatase 1 (ACP1) at chromosome 2p. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 728
retrieved_docs.content: The choroid (choroidea) is the anatomic locus for a wide spectrum of ocular disorders, encompassing inflammatory, degenerative, traumatic, vascular, hemorrhagic, ischemic and hereditary processes. Inflammatory entities that involve the choroid include disseminated choroiditis, generalized disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis, peripheral focal retinitis with retinochoroiditis, post‑inflammatory chorioretinal or choroidal scars, and post‑traumatic chorioretinal and choroidal scars. Vascular and hemorrhagic manifestations comprise choroidal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي), serous choroidal detachment (انفصال المشيمية المصلية), choroidal detachment of the eye (انفصال مشيمية العين), and hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي). Degenerative changes are described as partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, calcific degeneration (تنكس حصوي), general choroidal degeneration (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), secondary choroidal atrophy spread (انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية), and familial choroidal atrophy (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية). Specific inflammatory‑ischemic disease such as acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy (APMPPE; اعتلال الظهارة الصباغي اللوحي متعدد البؤر الخلفي الحاد) also localizes to the choroid. Sclerotic alterations around the optic disc are reported as partial peripapillary choroidal sclerosis (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي), total peripapillary choroidal sclerosis (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي), and hereditary choroidal sclerosis (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي). Additionally, choroidal lesions can present as retinal choroidal scars (ندبة مشيمية شبكية), underscoring the need for thorough choroidal assessment in diagnosing and managing these diverse ocular pathologies.

Inflammatory and infectious processes predominantly affect

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 698
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is repeatedly identified as the primary anatomic site for a broad spectrum of ocular pathologies. Inflammatory disorders localized to the choroid include disseminated choroiditis, focal choroiditis, generalized disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis, peripheral disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis, metastatic disseminated retinitis‑retinochoroiditis, as well as combined entities such as التهاب المشيمية (choroiditis), التهاب المشيمية والشبكية (choroiditis‑retinitis), and the unspecified focal variant التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد. Post‑inflammatory and post‑traumatic sequelae—post‑inflammatory chorioretinal scar, post‑inflammatory choroid scar, and post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar—are also situated in the choroidal tissue. Degenerative and structural changes affecting the choroidea comprise تنكس حصوي (calcific degeneration), تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة (choroidal degeneration), and choroidal sclerosis around the optic disc in both partial (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي) and total (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) forms. Vascular complications recorded as choroidal findings include نزيف مشيمي باثق (persistent choroidal hemorrhage), hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي), and non‑hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين). The choroidea is additionally noted to have a relationship with بُقَعُ روث, although the clinical significance of this association is not detailed. Moreover, the choroidea is described in Arabic as “حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي,” indicating that pathological observations may be localized to a specific genetic chorionic region. Recognizing the choroid as the common locus for these inflammatory, infectious, traumatic, degenerative, vascular, and post‑injury scarring processes—and for its genetic and ancillary associations—is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted imaging, and appropriate therapeutic planning in ophthalmic practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1257
retrieved_docs.content: The choroid (choroidea) serves as the primary anatomic site for a wide spectrum of ocular inflammatory, degenerative, and postoperative conditions. Various forms of choroiditis and chorioretinitis—including disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis of the posterior pole, focal choroiditis, generalized disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis, and juxtapapillary focal choroiditis and chorioretinitis—are all localized to the choroid. Degenerative processes such as partial generalized choroidal dystrophy also manifest within this vascular layer. Healing and sequelae of inflammation or surgery are represented by post‑inflammatory chorioretinal scar, post‑inflammatory choroid scar, and postsurgical choroid scar, each indicating that scar formation occurs in the choroidal tissue. Specific disease entities that involve the choroid include acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy (APMPPE), congenital choroidal malformations (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), choroidal rupture (التمزق المشيمي), and various patterns of chorioretinal inflammation such as diffuse, focal, or unspecified retinal‑choroidal inflammation (التهاب الشبكية والمشيمية المنتشر، غير محدد; التهاب المشيمية; التهاب المشيمية والشبكية; التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد). Age‑related and secondary choroidal atrophy (الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية; انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية) as well as choroidal inversion (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة) and partial peripapillary choroidal calcification (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي) further illustrate the diverse pathological changes that can affect this structure. Additionally, the choroid is linked to the term بُقَعُ روث, indicating an ancillary relationship within the described dataset. Recognizing the choroid as the common locus for these varied conditions aids clinicians in diagnosis, monitoring, and targeted management of ocular disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1079
retrieved_docs.content: (isolated choroidal inflammation), التهاب المشيمية والشبكية (combined choroidal‑retinal inflammation), التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد (focal unspecified combined inflammation), and حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي (hereditary choroidal atrophy). Collectively, these conditions demonstrate the complex interplay of vascular occlusion, hemorrhage, inflammation, metabolic injury, and degenerative remodeling within the retina and choroid; recognizing each as a child of “Disorders of choroid and retina (H30‑H36)” aids clinicians in systematic diagnosis, risk stratification, and the selection of targeted therapeutic strategies to preserve visual function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1158
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is a central anatomic locus for a wide spectrum of ocular pathology and therapeutic intervention. Hemorrhagic involvement is represented by “نزيف مشيمي” (choroidal hemorrhage). Inflammatory and infectious processes localize to the choroid and include disseminated choroiditis, disseminated chorioretinitis of the posterior pole, “التهاب المشيمية” (choroiditis), “التهاب المشيمية والشبكية” (combined choroiditis and retinitis), and the nonspecific “التهاب الشبكية والمشيمية المنتشر، غير محدد” (unspecified diffuse retino‑choroiditis). Focal inflammatory lesions are described as juxtapapillary focal choroiditis and chorioretinitis and peripheral focal retinitis and retinochoroiditis. Immune‑mediated entities affecting the choroid comprise acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy (“اعتلال الظهارة الصباغي اللوحي متعدد البؤر الخلفي الحاد”, APMPPE) and familial choroidal involvement (“الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية”). Structural and degenerative

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 1112
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is the principal anatomic substrate for a broad spectrum of ocular disorders, encompassing hemorrhagic, inflammatory, dystrophic, fibrotic, degenerative, and calcific pathologies. Acute and massive choroidal hemorrhages (نزيف مشيمي, نزيف مشيمي باثق) represent direct hemorrhagic lesions of the choroid. Numerous inflammatory entities localize to this layer, including disseminated choroiditis, chorioretinitis of the posterior pole, peripheral disseminated choroiditis, peripheral focal retinitis, generalized or partial generalized choroidal dystrophies, choroiditis (التهاب المشيمية), combined choroiditis‑retinitis (التهاب المشيمية والشبكية), and focal unspecified choroid‑retinal inflammation (التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد). Post‑injury and post‑procedure sequelae also manifest as fibrotic remodeling within the choroid, such as post‑inflammatory chorioretinal scars, post‑inflammatory choroid scars, postsurgical choroid scars, and post‑traumatic choroid scars. Degenerative changes include secondary choroidal atrophy spread (انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية) and serous choroidal detachment (انفصال المشيمية المصلية). Specific pigment‑epithelium disorders, exemplified by acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy (APMPPE, اعتلال الظهارة الصباغي اللوحي متعدد البؤر الخلفي الحاد), and familial choroidal atrophy (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), further illustrate the choroid’s involvement. Additional degenerative and calcific alterations described in the choroidea are تنكس حصوي (stone‑related degeneration), تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة (degeneration of the chorionic tissue), and حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي (hereditary calcification of the chorionic structure). Collectively, these entities underscore the choroid’s central role in ocular disease, guiding diagnostic assessment and targeted therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 1236
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of the choroid and retina (ICD‑10 H30‑H36) represent a broad diagnostic category that encompasses a wide spectrum of posterior‑segment ocular conditions, including inflammatory, vascular, degenerative, traumatic, and hereditary/metabolic entities. Inflammatory subtypes comprise disseminated choroiditis, focal choroiditis, unspecified focal choroid‑retinal inflammation, and acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy (APMPPE), illustrating immune‑mediated attacks on the choroid and retinal pigment epithelium. Vascular and hemorrhagic disorders include retinal phlebitis (flebitis), choroidal hemorrhage and massive choroidal hemorrhage, hypertensive retinopathy, proliferative diabetic retinopathy, microvascular diabetic retinal disease, and cotton‑wool spots, reflecting systemic vascular pathology manifesting in the retina and choroid. Degenerative changes are captured by partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, senile choroidal atrophy, and peripheral focal retinitis/retinochoroiditis, indicating progressive loss of choroidal and retinal structure. Traumatic sequelae such as post‑inflammatory chorioretinal scar, post‑traumatic choroidal scar, choroidal detachment, and hemorrhagic choroidal detachment demonstrate how injury can produce lasting structural compromise. Hereditary and metabolic calcific disorders are represented by choroidal calcification around the globe and hereditary choroidal calcification, highlighting genetic contributions to choroidal pathology. Additionally, retinal membranous scar (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) is classified as a child entity within this overarching group, underscoring its status as a distinct sub‑type of choroidal and retinal disorders. Recognizing all of these entities as subcategories of “Disorders of choroid and retina” facilitates systematic diagnosis, appropriate imaging, and targeted management of posterior‑segment diseases.
